prompt: Which detail best supports the idea that the octopus cannot perceive its own display?

choices.text: the statement that the octopus is colorblind and cannot see the hues it makes
choices.rationale: Correct. Colorblindness directly explains why it cannot see the hues it makes.

choices.text: the statement that each sac of pigment is ringed by a band of muscle
choices.rationale: The muscle ring explains how color appears, not whether the animal sees it.

choices.text: the statement that the octopus has no bones and almost no color of its own
choices.rationale: Lacking bones is unrelated to perceiving color.

choices.text: the statement that its color can bloom or vanish in less than a second
choices.rationale: Speed of change does not address perception.

explanation: The claim about perception is supported specifically by the detail that the animal is colorblind.

stimulus.title: The Color of Thought
stimulus.text: An octopus has no bones and, oddly, almost no color of its own. Yet in less than a second it can flush deep red with alarm or fade to match the speckled gray of a stone. Beneath its skin lie millions of tiny sacs of pigment, each ringed by muscle. When the muscles pull, the sacs widen and color blooms; when they relax, the color vanishes. Stranger still, the octopus is colorblind, so it cannot see the very hues it produces. Some researchers suspect its skin itself senses light, letting the animal paint a disguise it can never admire.

prompt: Which detail best supports the claim that maps improved as exploration advanced?

choices.text: the statement that a finished, accurate map can hide how hard it was to make
choices.rationale: Hidden difficulty is a reflection, not evidence of improvement.

choices.text: the statement that some old maps added sea monsters as a form of decoration
choices.rationale: Decorative monsters do not show improvement.

choices.text: the statement that, as explorers returned with measurements, the guesses were slowly corrected
choices.rationale: Correct. Corrections following explorers' measurements show improvement over time.

choices.text: the statement that mapmakers filled blank spaces with mountains they imagined
choices.rationale: Imagined mountains describe the early errors, not the improvement.

explanation: Improvement is supported by corrections from returning explorers.

stimulus.title: The Edge of the Map
stimulus.text: Old maps are full of guesses. Where knowledge ran out, mapmakers filled the blank spaces with mountains they had never seen and coastlines that bent the wrong way. Some added sea monsters, less from belief than from a need to decorate the unknown. As explorers returned with measurements, the guesses were slowly corrected, and the monsters swam off the page. But a finished, accurate map can hide how hard it was to make. Every clean line was once a question, settled only by someone willing to sail past the edge of what was known and come back with an answer.

prompt: Which detail best supports the idea that writing helped Tomas understand his own feelings?

choices.text: the detail that the first version listed the small triumphs of his new city
choices.rationale: The cheerful list hid, rather than revealed, his feelings.

choices.text: the detail that he wrote the letter three times before stopping
choices.rationale: Writing it three times shows effort, not the self-understanding itself.

choices.text: the detail that he kept the third version folded in a drawer
choices.rationale: Keeping the letter in a drawer does not by itself show understanding.

choices.text: the detail that writing the words let him admit how far from home he felt
choices.rationale: Correct. This directly states that the writing let him admit his feelings.

explanation: Self-understanding is shown by the line about admitting how far from home he felt.

stimulus.title: The Unsent Letter
stimulus.text: Tomas wrote the letter three times before he stopped trying to send it. In the first version he was cheerful, listing the small triumphs of his new city as if homesickness were a thing he had outgrown. In the second he was honest, and the page filled with everything he missed until it embarrassed him. The third version said almost nothing at all. He kept that one folded in a drawer. Months later he found it again and realized the words he could not send had still done their work: writing them had let him admit, if only to himself, how far from home he truly felt.

prompt: Which detail best supports the claim that the cold preserves the seeds' ability to grow?

choices.text: the statement that the seeds were gathered from around the world
choices.rationale: The seeds' origins do not address viability.

choices.text: the statement that even after decades the stored seeds can still sprout
choices.rationale: Correct. Sprouting after decades shows preservation of viability.

choices.text: the statement that the vault serves as a backup against crop loss
choices.rationale: Calling it a backup states purpose, not preservation.

choices.text: the statement that the vault is built deep inside a frozen mountain
choices.rationale: The location alone does not show preserved viability.

explanation: Preserved viability is shown by sprouting after decades.

stimulus.title: A Vault for Seeds
stimulus.text: Deep inside a frozen mountain, a vault holds something more valuable than gold: seeds. Hundreds of thousands of varieties of crops, gathered from around the world, rest in sealed packets at temperatures far below freezing. The cold slows the seeds' aging to a crawl, so that even after decades they can still sprout. The vault is a backup. If a war, flood, or disease wipes out a crop somewhere, a sample can be withdrawn and grown again. Its keepers hope it will rarely be needed, the way one hopes never to use a fire extinguisher. Its value lies precisely in being ready and almost never used.

prompt: Which detail best supports the claim that automation improved reliability?

choices.text: the statement that storms made the keeper's nightly work dangerous
choices.rationale: Dangerous storms concern the old hardship, not reliability gains.

choices.text: the statement that the keepers were gradually withdrawn over time
choices.rationale: Withdrawal of keepers is the human cost, not the reliability claim.

choices.text: the statement that the lights still shine, more reliably than ever before
choices.rationale: Correct. 'More reliably than ever' directly supports improved reliability.

choices.text: the statement that isolation made the work of the keeper lonely
choices.rationale: Loneliness describes the old role, not reliability.

explanation: Improved reliability is stated directly in that detail.

stimulus.title: Keeping the Light
stimulus.text: For centuries, a lighthouse meant a keeper. Someone had to climb the tower each night, trim the wick, polish the lens, and wind the clockwork that turned the light. Storms made the work dangerous, and isolation made it lonely, but ships depended on that faithful beam. In the twentieth century, automation arrived. Electric lamps, timers, and remote sensors took over the tasks one by one, and keepers were gradually withdrawn. The lights still shine, more reliably than ever, yet something human slipped away with the last keeper. The machines kept the promise of the light; they could not keep its company.

prompt: Which detail best supports the idea that Nadia's playing improved on the loaner?

choices.text: the detail that she returned the borrowed violin to its case afterward
choices.rationale: Returning the violin does not show improved playing.

choices.text: the detail that she had nearly refused the instrument to begin with
choices.rationale: Her near-refusal is about hesitation, not improvement.

choices.text: the detail that two of her school violin's pegs slipped every lesson
choices.rationale: Slipping pegs describe the old instrument, not improvement.

choices.text: the detail that notes she had fought for all year now arrived easily
choices.rationale: Correct. The ease of once-difficult notes shows improved playing.

explanation: Improvement is shown by once-hard notes arriving easily.

stimulus.title: The Borrowed Violin
stimulus.text: Nadia had practiced on a school instrument so battered that two of its pegs slipped during every lesson. When her teacher handed her a loaner violin for the recital, she nearly refused; it felt wrong to play something so fine. But as she drew the bow across the strings, the tone opened like a window onto a bright street. Notes she had fought for all year now arrived easily, as if the instrument had been waiting for her. Afterward, returning it to its case, she understood that her old struggles had not been wasted. The fine violin had simply revealed the player she had quietly become.

prompt: Which detail best supports the claim that current systems limit glass recycling?

choices.text: the statement that glass can be melted down and remade without losing quality
choices.rationale: Quality retention is a property of glass, not a system failure.

choices.text: the statement that a bottle recycled today can become a bottle next month
choices.rationale: This describes the ideal, not the limit.

choices.text: the statement that the material is willing while our systems need redesigning
choices.rationale: 'The material is willing' is figurative, not specific evidence.

choices.text: the statement that much glass ends up in landfills because collection is uneven
choices.rationale: Correct. Uneven collection sending glass to landfills shows a system limitation.

explanation: System limits are supported by the uneven-collection detail.

stimulus.title: The Endless Material
stimulus.text: Glass has a quiet superpower: it can be melted down and remade again and again without losing quality. A bottle recycled today can become a bottle next month, then another after that, in a loop that, in principle, never wears out. Compared with this ideal, reality lags. Much glass still ends up in landfills because collection is uneven and because mixing colors lowers the value of the recycled material. Sorting by color and keeping glass out of general trash would let more of it re-enter the loop. The material is willing; it is our systems, the author suggests, that need redesigning.

prompt: Which detail best supports the claim that marshes can keep pace with rising seas?

choices.text: the statement that marshes were once drained and filled without much thought
choices.rationale: Past draining concerns history, not keeping pace.

choices.text: the statement that roots beneath the mud store carbon
choices.rationale: Carbon storage is a separate benefit, not pace with the sea.

choices.text: the statement that the marsh can look like flat, muddy wasteland
choices.rationale: The marsh's plain appearance does not address rising seas.

choices.text: the statement that the grasses trap sediment, letting the marsh build itself upward
choices.rationale: Correct. Trapping sediment to build upward directly shows the marsh keeping pace with the sea.

explanation: Keeping pace with the sea is supported by the sediment-trapping detail.

stimulus.title: The Quiet Buffer
stimulus.text: Where the land meets the sea, a salt marsh can look like wasteland: flat, muddy, and crossed by winding channels. Yet this drab landscape does remarkable work. Its dense grasses slow incoming waves, blunting the force of storms before they reach homes inland. The same grasses trap sediment, letting the marsh build itself upward as the sea slowly rises. Beneath the mud, roots store carbon that might otherwise warm the air. For years, marshes were drained and filled without much thought. Only later did people recognize that the unglamorous marsh had been protecting them all along, asking nothing in return.
